title: GE Aerospace Isn't an Engine Maker. It's a Recurring Revenue Machine.
subtitle: 44,000 engines in service, $19.2 billion in deferred revenue, and 14 straight quarters of beating estimates. The aftermarket is the moat — and Pratt & Whitney's crisis is making it wider.
summary: GE Aerospace carries $19.2 billion in deferred service revenue from power-by-the-hour maintenance contracts on 44,000+ engines — an installed base that generates 20–30 years of recurring cash flows per engine. While the market prices GE as a cyclical aerospace manufacturer, the numbers tell a different story: a capital-light services machine with a 48.2% ROE and a widening competitive moat as Pratt & Whitney's GTF engine crisis drives airlines toward LEAP-powered narrowbodies.

image.caption: GE Aerospace LEAP engine on wing

links.label: Reuters — Pratt & Whitney GTF Engine Groundings Widen

videos.title: GE Aerospace CEO Larry Culp on forecast: Revenues will be up double digits

bodyHtml:
The market still thinks of GE Aerospace as an engine maker. That is the single most expensive misunderstanding in industrial investing right now. GE does not sell engines. GE sells 30-year service contracts that happen to come with a free engine attached. The 44,000 powerplants bolted onto wings are not inventory — they are annuity contracts generating maintenance revenue for two to three decades. And the numbers coming out of Cincinnati prove it.

Start with the ledger. GE Aerospace carries $19.2 billion in deferred service revenue — $18.1 billion current and $1.07 billion non-current — cash already collected from airlines for future work under power-by-the-hour contracts. That is $19.2 billion that ratchets down over time as GE performs the work, meaning the company knows exactly what it will earn for years ahead. No demand forecasting, no order cycle risk, no waiting for the next Boeing delivery schedule. The revenue is already in the bank. The only question is when GAAP lets you recognize it.

That predictability shows up in the free cash flow. GE generated $7.26 billion in FCF in fiscal 2025 — more than double the $3.68 billion from the year before. Q2 2026 alone delivered $2.86 billion, up 49% year over year. And here is the kicker: capital expenditure runs at just 2.5% of revenue. The business is capital-light because the installed base was paid for years ago. What you are seeing now is the cash yield on infrastructure already built. That is a tollbooth business hiding inside an aerospace label.

GE has beaten earnings estimates for 14 consecutive quarters — three and a half years of outperforming the sell-side, the longest streak in industrials. Q2 2026 delivered $2.02 per share against a $1.86 consensus — an 8.6% beat. Q1 came in 16.3% above expectations. This is not a company coasting on a legacy base; it is one benefiting from structural tailwinds that strengthen every quarter. The 48.2% return on equity and 17.7% net profit margin tell the same story: GE makes more profit per dollar of equity than almost any company its size in the S&P 500. Compare that to RTX at 8.0% margins and an 11.6% ROE and the gap is unmistakable.

The competitive dynamics are swinging harder in GE's favor. Pratt & Whitney's GTF engine — powering a significant share of the A320neo fleet — has been plagued by durability issues that grounded planes and forced airlines to rethink fleet planning. The powdered metal defect that emerged in 2023 cascaded into a multi-year inspection cycle that cost Pratt billions and eroded confidence. Every grounded GTF-equipped A320neo creates a slot for CFM International — the 50-50 joint venture between GE and Safran that builds the LEAP engine — to pick up market share on new deliveries. The LEAP is already the engine of choice on the Boeing 737 MAX and competes head-to-head with the GTF on the A320neo. As airlines grow wary of GTF reliability, the default move is to order LEAP. That is not just a tailwind for engine sales; it is a tailwind for the aftermarket that will generate cash for decades after delivery.

The installed base breaks down as roughly 18,000 commercial engines and 26,000 military engines, each requiring scheduled overhauls, hot-section inspections, and parts replacements on a fixed cadence. Commercial engines typically hit their first major overhaul around 6,000 flight cycles and need a performance restoration shop visit every 10 to 12 years. Every shop visit is a revenue event. The LEAP program alone — still early in its lifecycle with thousands of engines yet to hit heavy maintenance — represents a wave of aftermarket revenue that has not started to crest. This is a business engineered for compounding, designed to convert manufacturing wins into decades of service income.

At $348.83 a share and a market cap north of $362 billion, GE looks expensive. The forward P/E sits around 40 times earnings — undeniably a premium multiple. But premium multiples are what you pay for locked-in revenue visibility, widening moats, and a structural advantage getting stronger by the quarter. Analysts agree: the mean target is $397.86 across 21 analysts, with a Strong Buy consensus. The bull case is not about the next engine order. It is about the 44,000 engines already flying, each one burning fuel and racking up hours toward its next revenue-generating shop visit. That is the moat. That is the machine. And it is not slowing down.

Disclosure: The Signal holds no position in GE. Positions may change. This is not financial advice.
